(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
executive/legal 
322-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
February 14, 1985 
Herbert M. Rosenthal 
General Counsel 
The State Bar of California 
555 Franklin Street 
San Francisco, CA 
94102-4498 
Dear Mr. Rosenthal: 
Re: 
A-85-032 
... 
Commissioner Stanford has referred your letter of 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-64 .... 
February 7, 1985, to me. 
You are correct that, since the State 
Bar is now a "state agency" within the meaning of the Political 
Reform Act, employees of the State Bar are no longer covered by 
Chapter 6 of the Act. 
Government Code Section 86300(a). 
Since 
the status of the State Bar's lobbyists has been changed from 
that of "independent contractor" to that of "employee," neither 
the State Bar nor its lobbyists have any registration or 
reporting obligations under the Act, and the State Bar lobbyists 
are not subject to the prohibitions the Act places on lobbyists. 
I hope this answers your questions. 
BAM:nwm 
Ver~UIY YOur~\ 
Barbar~) 
General Counsel 


THE STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA 
CRITCIIFIELD, 
T!ItHL\S R. 
DIXO:\Q. 
Mr. Dan Stanford 
Chairman 
555 FRANKLIN STREET 
SA!'! FRANCISCO, CA 94102-4498 
(415) 561-8200 
7 February 1985 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1100 -K- Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear Mr. Stanford: 
It is my opinion, as General Counsel of the State Bar 
of California, that in light of the 1984 amendment of the 
Political Reform Act which includes courts and judicial branch 
agencies within the definition of "state agency" and recent 
changes in the employment status of the State Bar's legisla-
tive representatives, the lobbyist provisions of Chapter 6, 
Articles 1 and 2 of the Act will no longer be applicable to 
the State Bar vvhen the amendment becomes operative July 1, 
1985. 
Effective January 1, 1985 the employment status of the 
State Bar's lobbyists was changed from "independent contractor" 
status to that of "employee." 
Under the provisions of Government Code section 86300, 
other state agencies and their employees who attempt to influ-
ence legislation are exempt from the lobbyist registration, 
reporting, and prohibition provisions of the Act except that 
employees of these agencies are prohibited from making gifts 
of more than ten dollars in a calendar month to an elected 
state officer or legislative official. 
Government Code section 
82039, which defines "lobbyist" for purposes of the Act, 
expressly excludes from the definition individuals who are 
exempted under Government Code section 86300. 
Accordingly, 
if the State Bar is deemed to corne within the section 86300 
exemption, its employees who attempt to influence legislation 
will not be lobbyists and the State Bar will not be an employer 
of lobbyists 
Under these circumstances there would appear to 
be no basis for applying the lobbyist registration, reporting, 
and prohibition provisions to the State Bar. 


l1r. Dan Stanford 
7 February 1985 
Page 2 
-
l1oreover, as a "state agency" within the meaning of the 
Act, the Conflict of Interest Code provisions of the Act 
(Govt. Code § 87300 et seq.) and the reporting requirements 
of these provisions will be applicable to the State Bar. 
This 
would appear to further obviate the necessity of applying the 
lobbyist provisions to the State Bar. 
For all of the reasons mentioned I have concluded that 
the State Bar, like other state agencies, should be exempted 
from the lobbyist provisions of Chapter 6, Articles 1 and 2 of 
the Act when the amendment which makes the State Bar a "state 
agency" becomes operative. 
Please let me know if you are in 
agreement with this conclusion. 
Thank you for your consideration of this matter. 
I look 
forward to hearing from you. 
HMR:sd 
Very truly yours, 
Herbert 11. Rosenthal 
General Counsel 


TIIO:\V\S 
DlXON Q, 
THE STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA 
CRITClIFIELD, 
Ms. Barbara A. Milman 
General Counsel 
555 FRANKLIN STREET 
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94102-4498 
(415) 561-8200 
22 February 1985 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1100 -K- Street Building 
Sacramento, California 
95815 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
DO'> IV, ~IARTENS, 
~I;\RSI!A 
H. KE'>'>ETH '>ORIAN, 
RO'>ALD L. OLSO'>. Los 
JOO'> fIEE RIIO, Los Angeles 
PHILIP ~1 SCHAFER, 
THO~lAS F. S~lEGAL. JR" San 
DA:'\IELJ. 
La Alesa 
HOWARD K. 
, Sacramento 
Thank you for your letter of February 14, 1985 stating that you concur 
with my conclusion that since the State Bar is now a "state agency" within 
the meaning of the Political Reform Act, and since the status of the State 
Bar's lobbyists has been changed from "independent contractor" to that of 
"employee", the State Bar and its employees are no longer covered by 
Chapter 6 of the Act. 
Accordingly, I have advised our lobbyists that 
neither they nor the State Bar have any registration or reporting obliga-
tions under the Act and that the lobbyists are not subject to the prohibi-
tion the Act places on lobbyists. 
I appreciate your prompt response in this matter. 
HMR: sd 
cc: 
Terry Flanigan 
Stanley Wieg 
Judith Harper 
Very truly yours, 
Herbert M. Rosenthal 
General Counsel

sections.conclusion: You are correct that, since the State Bar is now a "state agency" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, employees of the State Bar are no longer covered by Chapter 6 of the Act. Government Code Section 86300(a). Since the status of the State Bar's lobbyists has been changed from that of "independent contractor" to that of "employee," neither the State Bar nor its lobbyists have any registration or reporting obligations under the Act, and the State Bar lobbyists are not subject to the prohibitions the Act places on lobbyists.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the State Bar of California, now classified as a state agency under the 1984 amendment to the Political Reform Act, and with its former independent contractor lobbyists now reclassified as employees, remain subject to the lobbyist registration, reporting, and prohibition provisions of Chapter 6 of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that the State Bar, now classified as a state agency under the 1984 Political Reform Act amendment, and its employees (formerly independent contractor lobbyists) are exempt from Chapter 6 lobbyist registration, reporting, and prohibition provisions. The State Bar and its lobbyists have no registration or reporting obligations under these provisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters: (1) incoming request from State Bar GC Rosenthal to FPPC (Feb 7, 1985), (2) FPPC response letter from Barbara Milman, General Counsel (Feb 14, 1985), and (3) follow-up from State Bar GC acknowledging FPPC agreement (Feb 22, 1985). Extracted as advice_letter based on the FPPC's formal response. No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but content clearly identifies the legal issue and FPPC's answer.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the State Bar of California, now classified as a state agency under the 1984 amendment to the Political Reform Act, and with its former independent contractor lobbyists now reclassified as employees, remain subject to the lobbyist registration, reporting, and prohibition provisions of Chapter 6 of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: You are correct that, since the State Bar is now a "state agency" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, employees of the State Bar are no longer covered by Chapter 6 of the Act. Government Code Section 86300(a). Since the status of the State Bar's lobbyists has been changed from that of "independent contractor" to that of "employee," neither the State Bar nor its lobbyists have any registration or reporting obligations under the Act, and the State Bar lobbyists are not subject to the prohibitions the Act places on lobbyists.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter (A-85-032) responding to the State Bar of California's General Counsel regarding whether the State Bar remains subject to Political Reform Act lobbyist provisions after being reclassified as a state agency and changing its lobbyists' employment status from independent contractors to employees. The FPPC affirms the State Bar's conclusion that it and its employees are now exempt from Chapter 6 lobbyist provisions.
